title: Aggregation and beyond: Some basic issues on the prediction of behavior
venue: Journal of Personality
research_question: When does aggregating observations improve the reliability, validity, and generalizability of predictions about broad dispositions?
method: Methodological analysis of aggregation over stimuli, situations, occasions, judges, behavioral items, and participants, with implications drawn from the behavioral-prediction literature.
population_or_data: No new participant sample; the paper analyzes principles and prior evidence concerning prediction from single versus aggregated behavioral observations.
core_finding: Single acts are usually too narrow and unreliable to represent broad traits; appropriate aggregation can reduce occasion- and situation-specific error, while poorly chosen aggregation can erase information and lower validity.
architecture_implications: Require repeated evidence across occasions and contexts before updating a broad trait estimate from interaction behavior.
Retain event-level state and context records so aggregation does not hide stable contingencies or meaningful variability.
limitations: This is a methodological synthesis rather than a new digital-interaction study, and it does not specify a universal observation count; the value of aggregation depends on sampling the intended behavioral domain.

search_queries: PubMed Epstein aggregation prediction behavior personality 1983
"Aggregation and beyond" 10.1111/j.1467-6494.1983.tb00338.x

title: Toward a structure- and process-integrated view of personality: Traits as density distributions of states
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Can stable personality traits coexist with substantial within-person variation in trait-relevant states during everyday life?
method: Three experience-sampling studies repeatedly measured Big Five-relevant states over two to three weeks and examined within-person distributions, stable individual differences, and situational reactivity.
population_or_data: Participants in three intensive experience-sampling studies reporting everyday Big Five-relevant behavior across two to three weeks.
core_finding: Individuals routinely expressed a wide range of trait-relevant states, yet their average state levels and distributional features were highly stable; some variability, including extraversion shifts, tracked situational cues.
architecture_implications: Represent a person with a distribution over states, including a central tendency and variability, rather than one fixed trait label.
Condition state estimates on situation and preserve uncertainty instead of treating every message as direct evidence of the same enduring disposition.
limitations: The observation windows were only two to three weeks and relied on repeated self-reports; the study did not test whether language or messaging traces recover the same state distributions over longer periods.

search_queries: PubMed Fleeson traits density distributions states 2001
"Toward a structure- and process-integrated view of personality"

title: The independent effects of personality and situations on real-time expressions of behavior and emotion
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: How much do person-level traits and contemporaneous situation characteristics independently explain real-time behavior and emotion?
method: Experience sampling combined trait assessment with repeated real-time measures of situation characteristics, behavior, and emotion, then modeled person and situation effects together.
population_or_data: 210 participants providing intensive real-time reports of situations, behavior, and emotion.
core_finding: Personality traits and situation characteristics each independently predicted momentary behavior and emotion, supporting an additive account rather than a trait-only explanation of observed conduct.
architecture_implications: Store situation descriptors and interaction context alongside behavioral evidence so a transient expression is not automatically attributed to personality.
Expose separate person-effect and context-effect contributions, with uncertainty, when explaining a digital-brain inference.
limitations: The study used an intensive but bounded self-report design and did not test language-model transfer; additive average effects do not establish that a specific message reveals a stable trait or its cause.

search_queries: PubMed Sherman Rauthmann personality situations real-time behavior emotion
"The independent effects of personality and situations" 10.1037/pspp0000036

title: Linguistic styles: Language use as an individual difference
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Are computerized word-use measures reliable individual differences, and do they relate to personality, behavior, and other external criteria?
method: Multiple studies assessed internal consistency, factor structure, replication, and convergent associations of computerized word categories across diaries, prompted writing, abstracts, essays, self-reports, behavioral measures, and health markers.
population_or_data: Samples included 15 inpatients' diaries, 35 students' writing, 40 psychologists' abstracts, essays from 838 students with a 381-student replication, a 79-student multimethod comparison, and self-report or health data from more than 1,200 students.
core_finding: Several language-use dimensions were internally consistent and a four-factor linguistic structure replicated across writing samples, but associations with personality and external measures were generally modest.
architecture_implications: Treat lexical style as a noisy auxiliary measurement channel, not as a deterministic personality label.
Estimate reliability from sufficient text across occasions and validate language features against independent owner and behavioral criteria before use.
limitations: The studies mixed small and heterogeneous, often prompted samples; category counts omit semantics and interaction context, and modest associations do not establish a unique psychological cause for a word pattern.

search_queries: PubMed Pennebaker King linguistic styles individual difference 1999
"Linguistic styles: language use as an individual difference"

title: Personality in its natural habitat: Manifestations and implicit folk theories of personality in daily life
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: How are Big Five traits expressed and perceived in naturally occurring daily activities, interactions, moods, and language?
method: Participants wore the Electronically Activated Recorder for two days; Big Five scores and observer impressions were correlated with coded ambient snippets covering interactions, locations, activities, moods, and language.
population_or_data: 96 participants tracked for two days with intermittent ambient-audio samples and independent impressions based on those recordings.
core_finding: Many naturalistic behaviors and language cues aligned with trait definitions, but patterns were often gender-specific and openness was an exception; judges' impressions were most accurate for visible extraversion cues.
architecture_implications: Model observable social reputation separately from private self-concept because interaction traces preferentially expose visible traits.
Audit gender and context interactions before interpreting daily communication cues as personality evidence.
limitations: The sample was small, the observation period was two days, and ambient recordings expose only sampled public behavior; gender-specific findings and weak openness signals limit generalization to private text conversations or inner experience.

search_queries: PubMed Mehl Gosling Pennebaker personality natural habitat 2006
"Personality in its natural habitat" EAR

title: Thin slices of behavior as cues of personality and intelligence
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: How accurate and cross-situationally consistent are personality and intelligence judgments made from brief behavioral episodes?
method: Self-reports, peer reports, intelligence tests, and observer ratings from 15 videotaped episodes were compared; analyses examined cross-situational consistency, stereotypes, information overlap, and gains from aggregating episodes.
population_or_data: 600 participants, each represented by 15 brief videotaped behavioral episodes plus self-report, peer-report, and intelligence criteria.
core_finding: Trait impressions showed moderate cross-situational consistency, and their agreement with knowledgeable informants improved as more episodes were combined, with little additional gain after about six episodes; judges also lacked overlapping information.
architecture_implications: Require multiple interaction episodes before forming an observer-style personality estimate and expose the evidence coverage behind it.
Separate a model of how the person appears to others from claims about self-knowledge, and audit demographic cues that can act as shortcuts.
limitations: Structured video episodes are not private messaging, observer agreement is not direct access to latent personality, and the saturation point for these tasks cannot be assumed to apply to text, relationships, or cultures.

search_queries: PubMed Borkenau thin slices behavior personality intelligence 2004
"Thin slices of behavior as cues of personality and intelligence"

title: Using Linguistic Cues for the Automatic Recognition of Personality in Conversation and Text
venue: Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research
research_question: How accurately can Big Five traits be recognized from written and spoken language using different targets, feature sets, and statistical formulations?
method: Classification, regression, and ranking models used LIWC, lexical, prosodic, and conversational features from a large essay corpus and a smaller naturalistic speech corpus, with self-reported and observer-rated Big Five targets.
population_or_data: 2,479 psychology-student essays totaling 1.9 million words and Electronically Activated Recorder speech from 96 participants totaling 97,468 words and 15,269 utterances; the speech sample also had ratings from 18 observers.
core_finding: Some trait models beat baselines and ranking generally performed best, but performance depended strongly on trait, modality, feature set, and target; in the small speech corpus, observer-target models worked better while self-report models did not beat baseline.
architecture_implications: Declare whether an inference target is self-concept or observer reputation, because the same interaction cues do not measure them interchangeably.
Use trait- and domain-specific calibration with abstention; do not deploy one generic personality classifier across dialogue settings.
limitations: The conversational sample had only 96 people, the essays were prompted student writing, useful accuracy and cross-domain transfer were unresolved, and this paper reuses the Pennebaker and Mehl corpora rather than providing independent data.

search_queries: JAIR Mairesse Walker Mehl Moore personality conversation text 2007
"Using Linguistic Cues for the Automatic Recognition of Personality"

title: Personality in 100,000 Words: A large-scale analysis of personality and word use among bloggers
venue: Journal of Research in Personality
research_question: Which category-level and individual-word patterns in long-form naturalistic blogs correlate with Big Five domains and lower-order facets?
method: Spearman correlations linked LIWC categories and individual word frequencies to IPIP Big Five and facet scores; split-half analyses assessed lexical reliability and prior category associations were directly compared for replication.
population_or_data: 694 Blogger authors averaging 115,423 words over 23.9 months; 576 had Big Five scores and 431 also had facet scores.
core_finding: Large writing samples produced reliable word-use estimates and replicated a meaningful subset of earlier language-personality associations, while also revealing many specific correlates; openness had substantially stronger language associations than the other traits.
architecture_implications: Track evidence volume and per-feature reliability because sparse words and short histories cannot support the same inference as long longitudinal text.
Keep topic choice, lexical style, demographics, and platform selection as possible alternative explanations instead of interpreting a correlation as a trait mechanism.
limitations: The self-selected Blogger sample was 75% female, simple word counts ignored semantics and context, response and platform selection limit representativeness, and the exploratory correlations do not establish causal or out-of-platform validity.

search_queries: PMC Yarkoni Personality in 100,000 Words bloggers
"Personality in 100,000 Words" 10.1016/j.jrp.2010.04.001

title: Personality, Gender, and Age in the Language of Social Media: The Open-Vocabulary Approach
venue: PLOS ONE
research_question: Can open-vocabulary words, phrases, and topics reveal and predict personality and demographic variation beyond closed-vocabulary lexicons?
method: Differential language analysis extracted words, phrases, and topics from Facebook status updates; regressions adjusted associations for age and gender, and out-of-sample models compared open-vocabulary features with LIWC categories.
population_or_data: About 75,000 English-speaking Facebook volunteers with at least 1,000 status words, contributing more than 15.4 million messages and 700 million word, phrase, and topic instances plus personality questionnaires.
core_finding: Open-vocabulary features distinguished personality, age, and gender and carried more out-of-sample predictive information than fixed LIWC categories, but personality associations were trait-dependent and language patterns were also strongly demographic and contextual.
architecture_implications: Estimate nuisance effects such as age, language variety, and relationship context before assigning a language pattern to personality.
Version language models by platform and population, and attach source-domain limits to every generated trait estimate.
limitations: The self-selected Facebook sample excluded low-volume writers and adults over 65, only age and gender were modeled as key covariates, correlations were not causal, and the authors explicitly cautioned that language findings may not generalize across domains.

search_queries: PLOS Schwartz Eichstaedt Personality Gender Age Language Social Media 2013
"Open-Vocabulary Approach" Facebook personality 75,000

title: Automatic personality assessment through social media language
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Do language-based Big Five scores show convergent, discriminant, incremental, external, and temporal validity?
method: Open-vocabulary models were trained from Facebook language and questionnaire scores, then evaluated in a separate sample against self-reports, informant reports, trait discrimination, external correlates, and six-month test-retest stability.
population_or_data: 66,732 Facebook users for model development and a separate 4,824-user evaluation sample with language, Big Five self-reports, and subsets of informant or external-criterion data.
core_finding: Language-based scores converged with self- and informant reports, discriminated among traits, added some validity beyond informants, reproduced external-correlation patterns, and were stable across six-month windows.
architecture_implications: Validate any language-derived trait against multiple owner-approved criteria and report reliability over time rather than presenting an unqualified profile.
Use language assessment as a revisable complementary signal, not a replacement for explicit self-report or owner correction.
limitations: The labels and validation came from Facebook volunteers in the myPersonality ecosystem, stability covered six months rather than life-course change, and performance does not establish causal mechanisms, private motives, or transport to other platforms and relationships.

search_queries: PubMed Automatic personality assessment social media language Park 2015
"Automatic personality assessment through social media language" 10.1037/pspp0000020

title: Domain Adaptation from User-level Facebook Models to County-level Twitter Predictions
venue: Proceedings of the Eighth International Joint Conference on Natural Language Processing (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Can a user-level Facebook personality model be transferred to county-level Twitter language without labeled target data, and how can such transfer be checked?
method: Target Side Domain Adaptation adjusted county outlier frequencies and Facebook-Twitter frequency shifts, then evaluated predicted Big Five aggregates using distributional plausibility, year-to-year stability, limited state-level ground truth, and external county outcomes.
population_or_data: Facebook models trained from 65,896 users with status text and personality questionnaires; more than 150 million geolocated tweets from 2012-2014 covering 2,197 to 2,651 U.S. counties per year.
core_finding: Naive cross-platform and cross-level application produced implausible extreme scores and spurious associations; target-side adaptation reduced outliers, improved temporal stability, and generally improved expected external correlations.
architecture_implications: Block personality-model reuse across platforms, relationships, languages, or aggregation levels until target-domain drift and validity checks pass.
Monitor score distributions, temporal stability, and known-null relationships, and abstain when a new local meme or vocabulary shift drives an estimate.
limitations: Most counties lacked direct personality labels, so several checks relied on expected distributions and correlates; ecological county results cannot validate individual inference, and the U.S. Facebook-to-Twitter setting is narrow.

search_queries: ACL Anthology personality domain adaptation Facebook Twitter county
"Domain Adaptation from User-level Facebook Models to County-level Twitter Predictions"

title: Private traits and attributes are predictable from digital records of human behavior
venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
research_question: How accurately can psychodemographic attributes, including Big Five traits, be predicted from sparse Facebook Like records?
method: Singular-value decomposition represented user-Like data, followed by linear or logistic regression with 10-fold cross-validation against questionnaire, survey, and profile criteria.
population_or_data: 58,466 U.S. myPersonality volunteers with an average of 170 Likes; Big Five scores from a 20-item IPIP instrument were available for 54,373 users.
core_finding: Likes predicted openness and extraversion more strongly than the other Big Five traits, with accuracy increasing as more Likes were observed; the same traces also encoded many demographic and sensitive attributes.
architecture_implications: Treat behavior-trace models as proxy-rich predictors that may exploit demographics, interests, and social structure rather than a unique personality mechanism.
Require explicit consent, sensitive-attribute safeguards, confound audits, and calibrated uncertainty before any behavioral inference enters a digital brain.
limitations: The sample was self-selected, U.S.-based, and drawn from one Facebook application; the short personality criterion and random cross-validation do not establish temporal or cross-platform validity, and correlated Likes do not explain why a trait score is predicted.

search_queries: PMC Kosinski Stillwell Graepel private traits digital records 2013
"Private traits and attributes are predictable" PNAS

title: Computer-based personality judgments are more accurate than those made by humans
venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
research_question: How do Facebook-Like personality judgments compare with judgments by acquaintances on self-other agreement, interjudge agreement, and external validity?
method: LASSO models with 10-fold cross-validation predicted Big Five self-ratings from Likes and were compared with friend ratings and 13 external criteria.
population_or_data: 86,220 volunteers completed a 100-item personality measure; Like-based estimates were available for 70,520, one-friend ratings for 17,622, and two-friend ratings for 14,410 participants.
core_finding: At the average available Like count, computer scores agreed more with self-ratings than a single friend's brief rating and showed higher external validity on most tested outcomes; computer and human judgments also retained largely distinct information after accounting for self-ratings.
architecture_implications: Maintain separate trace-based, self-reported, and observer-reputation views instead of collapsing agreement with one criterion into a claim of true identity.
Compare inference channels under equivalent information and measurement conditions before declaring one more accurate.
limitations: The computer had many Likes while friends answered a 10-item instrument, self-ratings anchored the main accuracy criterion, all data came from the Facebook myPersonality setting, and Big Five accuracy does not imply a complete or privileged model of a person.

search_queries: PMC computer-based personality judgments more accurate humans Youyou 2015
"Computer-based personality judgments" PNAS 1418680112

title: Predicting personality from patterns of behavior collected with smartphones
venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
research_question: Which Big Five domains and facets can be predicted out of sample from passively logged smartphone behavior, and which behavioral classes contribute?
method: Elastic-net and random-forest models used 1,821 predictors from six behavior classes, with nested and repeated cross-validation, corrected significance tests, and permutation-based feature interpretation.
population_or_data: 624 predominantly young, highly educated volunteers using Android phones in Germany, observed for 30 days with 25,347,089 logging events and a 300-item German Big Five measure.
core_finding: Behavior predicted 57% of tested domains and facets with moderate correlations, led by communication and social metadata, but predictability varied sharply by construct and agreeableness was not predicted at all.
architecture_implications: Calibrate and report inference quality separately for every trait or facet; a successful model for sociability is not evidence for a general personality decoder.
Prefer minimally sufficient, consented metadata and retain an explicit no-inference state when a criterion is not predictably observed.
limitations: The sample was young, educated, German, and Android-only, observation lasted 30 days, behavior was compared with self-reported trait scores, age and gender were not model predictors, and out-of-sample performance within this sample does not prove cultural or device transfer.

search_queries: PMC Stachl predicting personality behavior collected smartphones 2020
"Predicting personality from patterns of behavior collected with smartphones"

title: Smartphone usage in the 21st century: who is active on WhatsApp?
venue: BMC Research Notes
research_question: How do directly logged WhatsApp-use duration, demographics, and Big Five self-reports relate in everyday smartphone use?
method: A consented Android app logged visual app sessions for at least four weeks; daily WhatsApp duration was correlated with age, gender, education, and a 10-item Big Five measure.
population_or_data: 2,418 media-recruited users, probably mostly in Germany, with a mean age of 24.64 years; 2,281 showed WhatsApp activity.
core_finding: WhatsApp duration had small associations with extraversion, conscientiousness, and neuroticism, no clear association with openness or agreeableness, and substantially stronger variation by age and gender.
architecture_implications: Do not turn messaging volume or duration into a global personality claim; demographic, life-stage, and opportunity variables can dominate the same trace.
Keep content inaccessible by default and distinguish low-risk usage metadata from any separately consented semantic analysis.
limitations: The nonrepresentative, mostly German sample used a two-item-per-trait BFI-10, recorded only visual-use duration rather than message counts or content, and supported correlations rather than causal or individual-level diagnostic inference.

search_queries: PMC WhatsApp personality actual usage Montag 2015
"Smartphone usage in the 21st century: who is active on WhatsApp?"

title: The developmental psychometrics of Big Five self-reports: Acquiescence, factor structure, coherence, and differentiation from ages 10 to 20
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: How do acquiescence, factor coherence, and differentiation in Big Five self-reports change from late childhood through early adulthood?
method: Year-by-year psychometric analyses of a large, diverse Internet sample examined response-style variability, within-domain coherence, between-domain differentiation, factor structure, and item comprehension from ages 10 to 20.
population_or_data: A large self-selected Internet sample of respondents aged 10 through 20 completing Big Five self-reports.
core_finding: Younger respondents showed much greater acquiescent response variation, and Big Five reports generally became more coherent within domains and more differentiated between domains with age, with distinct trajectories by trait.
architecture_implications: Treat age, comprehension, and response style as measurement conditions, not personality, when calibrating an owner questionnaire or language-derived target.
Do not assume one scoring model or threshold is invariant across developmental stages.
limitations: The evidence is cross-sectional and Internet self-selected, uses one questionnaire and administration mode, and cannot separate every age effect from cohort or selection; it concerns self-report psychometrics rather than message-based inference.

search_queries: PubMed Soto developmental psychometrics Big Five ages 10 20
"The developmental psychometrics of Big Five self-reports"

title: Testing the measurement equivalence of personality adjective items across cultures
venue: Journal of Research in Personality
research_question: Do Big Five Mini-Marker adjective scales have equivalent factor meanings and score origins across U.S., Greek, and Chinese samples?
method: Mean and Covariance Structures and confirmatory factor analyses tested configural, metric, and scalar equivalence, followed by item-level bias analysis.
population_or_data: Two U.S. undergraduate samples of 727 and 712 respondents, plus 991 Greek and 433 Chinese university students completing the 40-item Big Five Mini-Markers.
core_finding: All scales supported a broadly similar configuration, but metric and scalar invariance failed and several adjectives showed item-level bias, undermining direct comparison of observed scores across cultures.
architecture_implications: Require at least metric and scalar invariance evidence before comparing trait magnitudes across language or cultural groups.
Store item- and feature-level bias diagnostics; matching a broad factor shape is insufficient to justify a common scoring scale.
limitations: The study tested one adjective instrument in university samples, with culture, country, and language intertwined; failure for these Mini-Markers does not imply that every Big Five instrument or behavioral model is noninvariant.

search_queries: ScienceDirect Nye Roberts Saucier Zhou measurement equivalence personality adjective cultures
"Testing the measurement equivalence of personality adjective items across cultures"

title: Correcting Big Five Personality Measurements for Acquiescence: An 18-Country Cross-Cultural Study
venue: European Journal of Personality
research_question: Does person-level correction for acquiescent responding improve recovery of the intended Big Five factor pattern across countries and education levels?
method: Big Five scores from representative adult samples in 18 countries were corrected for individual acquiescence and compared with an idealized factor-loading pattern across national contexts.
population_or_data: Large adult-population samples designed to be representative within each of 18 countries, spanning individualistic and non-individualistic settings.
core_finding: Acquiescence correction improved factor-pattern congruence in every country and reached acceptable correspondence throughout individualistic countries, but not consistently in non-individualistic countries.
architecture_implications: Estimate response styles as nuisance parameters when explicit questionnaires supervise or correct a digital-brain profile.
Do not treat a statistical correction as universal; revalidate the construct after correction in each target population.
limitations: The abstract does not establish full measurement invariance or criterion validity, and country-level individualism may stand in for multiple social and survey-process differences; results apply to questionnaire scoring, not directly to language traces.

search_queries: Wiley Rammstedt Kemper Borg correcting Big Five acquiescence 18 country
"Correcting Big Five Personality Measurements for Acquiescence" 10.1002/per.1894

title: Challenges to capture the big five personality traits in non-WEIRD populations
venue: Science Advances
research_question: Do commonly used Big Five survey items retain their intended structure and validity in low- and middle-income, less-educated, face-to-face survey settings?
method: A comprehensive psychometric analysis compared factor structure, internal consistency, acquiescence, enumerator effects, education gradients, and predictive patterns across face-to-face surveys, Internet samples, and a U.S. benchmark.
population_or_data: 29 face-to-face surveys with 94,751 respondents in 23 low- and middle-income countries, contrasted with 198,356 self-selected Internet respondents from overlapping countries; 31 datasets from 24 countries were analyzed overall.
core_finding: Common Big Five items often failed to recover their intended traits in the face-to-face survey data, while Internet samples from the same countries performed much better; acquiescence, education, enumerator interaction, translation, and administration mode jointly contributed.
architecture_implications: Validate measurement by language, education, administration mode, and interaction setting instead of treating country or culture as a single correction variable.
Preserve provenance for who elicited a signal and how, because interviewer or partner effects can become systematic model features.
limitations: The comparison mixes administration mode, language availability, education, incentives, and self-selection, so no single factor is causal; many face-to-face instruments were short, and the results do not show that personality constructs themselves are absent.

search_queries: PMC Challenges capture big five non-WEIRD populations Laajaj 2019
"Challenges to capture the big five personality traits" Science Advances

title: Cross-cultural examination of the Big Five Personality Trait Short Questionnaire: Measurement invariance testing and associations with mental health
venue: PLOS ONE
research_question: Does the Big Five Personality Trait Short Questionnaire function comparably across English and Spanish, countries, and genders, and retain criterion-related validity?
method: Exploratory structural equation modeling and ESEM-within-CFA tested configural through increasingly strict invariance, internal consistency, and associations with mental-health and substance-use criteria.
population_or_data: 2,158 college students from the United States (1,117), Argentina (353), and Spain (688) completing online questionnaires.
core_finding: The best-fitting model allowed many cross-loadings and generally supported reasonable or partial invariance across language, country, and gender, while some item parameters and criterion correlations still differed across groups.
architecture_implications: Prefer flexible measurement models and inspect cross-loadings rather than forcing every linguistic or questionnaire feature to represent only one trait.
Record the exact populations and partial-invariance exceptions under which cross-group score comparisons are defensible.
limitations: The evidence is limited to college students in three countries, the English version needed further replication, some language comparisons required partial rather than full invariance, and tested external criteria were concentrated in mental health and substance use.

search_queries: PLOS Mezquita Big Five Personality Trait Short Questionnaire measurement invariance 2019
"Cross-cultural examination of the Big Five Personality Trait Short Questionnaire"
